sentence: Subjective:
The patient, a 50-year-old male, reports being okay and feeling that his medications are helping.
He mentions being concerned about the number of medications he is taking.
He has a history of a heart attack six months ago and has been prescribed medications to manage his coronary artery disease.
He reports a complaint of dizziness when standing up quickly, which he attributes to the heart attack or his medication.
He also mentions a preference for chocolate chip cookies and has a history of diabetes.
He has not been following up with his nephrologist as recommended.
He is a fan of the San Francisco 49ers and has not been taking medications that could harm his kidneys.
He has been experiencing some puffiness in his legs.
Past medical history:
The patient has a history of coronary artery disease, which led to a heart attack six months ago.
He has been prescribed medications to manage this condition, including aspirin, brilinta, and crestor.
He also has a history of diabetes, which contributed to his heart attack.
He has stage three chronic kidney disease, which requires him to follow up with a nephrologist.
He has not been taking medications that could harm his kidneys.
Review of systems:
The patient reports a complaint of dizziness when standing up quickly, which he attributes to his heart attack or medication.
He also reports feeling down and lonely since his heart attack.
Current medications:
Aspirin 81mg daily for coronary artery disease
Brilinta 90mg twice daily for coronary artery disease
Crestor 40mg daily for high cholesterol
Metoprolol 50mg daily for coronary artery disease
Fish oil for coronary artery disease
Objective:
Vital signs:
Blood pressure: 127/80
Pulse rate: [NOT MENTIONED]
Oxygen saturation: [NOT MENTIONED]
Temperature: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam:
The patient's neck exam is unremarkable.
The patient's heart exam reveals a slight 3/6 systolic ejection murmur at the left base.
The patient's lower extremity exam reveals one plus nonpitting edema.
Test Results:
Kidney function test results show stable kidney function.
Hemoglobin A1c was reported to be approximately 60%.
Assessment / Problem List:
The patient has coronary artery disease, which is currently stable; chronic kidney disease, stage three; and diabetes.
The patient is experiencing mild depression and loneliness following his heart attack.
Problem list: Coronary artery disease; chronic kidney disease, stage three; diabetes; mild depression.
Plan:
I prescribed the following medications:
Metoprolol 50mg daily for coronary artery disease.
Crestor 40mg daily for high cholesterol.
Aspirin 81mg daily for coronary artery disease.
I referred the patient to cardiac rehab to help him become more active and confident in exercising again.
I also referred the patient to a dietitian for education on managing his diabetes.
I will contact the patient's nephrologist to schedule an appointment.
I will order another hemoglobin A1c test to monitor the patient's diabetes.
I will talk to the patient's primary care physician about adjusting his insulin regimen.
Follow-up:
The patient will follow up with his cardiologist in six months to monitor his coronary artery disease.
He will also follow up with his nephrologist to monitor his kidney disease.
The patient will see a psychiatrist to discuss his depression and loneliness.
